TDS/TCS Return Filing & Compliance in Coimbatore

Quarterly TDS returns (24Q, 26Q, 27Q, 27EQ), TCS compliance, lower-deduction certificates and Form 16/16A generation for Coimbatore employers and businesses.

TDS compliance is unforgiving on deadlines — late filing attracts a daily fee that adds up fast, and defaults show up directly on your TRACES compliance report. We manage quarterly TDS return filing, TCS compliance, and Form 16/16A generation for Coimbatore employers, contractors, and businesses making specified payments, keeping deduction, deposit, and return filing tightly aligned.

The TDS sections Coimbatore businesses encounter most

Beyond salary TDS under Section 192 (computed on projected annual income and deducted proportionately each month), the sections that come up most often in day-to-day business are Section 194C (payments to contractors — 1% for individual/HUF payees, 2% for others), Section 194J (professional and technical fees — generally 10%, or 2% for specified technical services and call-centre payments), Section 194-I (rent — 10% for land/building/furniture, 2% for plant and machinery), and Section 194Q (purchase of goods above ₹50 lakh from a single seller in a year, at 0.1%, applicable to buyers whose turnover exceeded ₹10 crore in the preceding year). Where 194Q and TCS under Section 206C(1H) both technically apply to the same transaction, the law gives 194Q precedence — the buyer deducts, and the seller doesn’t additionally collect — a coordination point that trips up businesses on both sides of high-value B2B transactions if it isn’t checked explicitly.

Non-PAN and invalid-PAN deductions attract a flat 20% rate under Section 206AA regardless of what the specific section would otherwise prescribe, and Section 206AB layers a further higher rate for deductees who haven’t filed their own income tax returns for the relevant preceding years — both are checks we run before finalising any deduction, not just something we discover afterward on TRACES.

What’s included

  • Form 24Q (salary TDS) quarterly filing
  • Form 26Q (non-salary payments — contractors, professionals, rent) filing
  • Form 27Q (payments to non-residents) filing
  • Form 27EQ (TCS returns) for applicable sellers
  • Form 16 and Form 16A generation and issuance
  • Lower/nil deduction certificate (Form 13) application support
  • Correction returns for challan or PAN mismatches

Our process

  1. Deduction mapping — We map every payment category — salary, contractor, rent, professional fees, commission, goods purchase — to the correct TDS section and rate, since misclassification is the most common cause of default notices.
  2. Challan payment tracking — TDS deposited monthly (by the 7th, or 30th for March) is tracked and matched against deductions to avoid short-payment defaults.
  3. Quarterly return preparation — 24Q, 26Q, 27Q, or 27EQ is prepared from your deduction data and validated through the File Validation Utility before submission.
  4. TRACES reconciliation — We check the filed return against TRACES for PAN errors, challan mismatches, or short deductions, correcting them proactively rather than waiting for a default notice.
  5. Certificate issuance — Form 16 (annual, for salary) and Form 16A (quarterly, for non-salary) are generated from TRACES and issued to deductees.

TCS: the other side of the coin

Tax Collected at Source applies to specific categories where the seller, not the buyer, is responsible for collecting an additional amount at the time of sale — sale of specified goods (scrap, minerals, timber, and, above ₹50 lakh from a single buyer, general goods under Section 206C(1H) where 194Q doesn’t already apply), overseas remittances under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme, sale of overseas tour packages, and motor vehicle sales above ₹10 lakh. For most Coimbatore trading and manufacturing businesses, the practical question is simply whether their sale volume to any single buyer crosses the ₹50 lakh threshold in a year, and whether that buyer has already deducted under 194Q (in which case the seller doesn’t also collect TCS on the same transaction) — we check this per major buyer relationship rather than applying a blanket rule.

Common mistakes in Coimbatore TDS/TCS compliance

The most frequent issue is simple misclassification — treating a payment as a professional fee (194J) when it should be a contract payment (194C), or vice versa, which changes both the rate and sometimes the applicable threshold. Short deduction from applying the wrong rate slab, particularly around the 194C individual-versus-company distinction, is a close second. We also regularly see businesses miss the interplay between 194Q and 206C(1H) entirely, either double-taxing a transaction or missing the obligation altogether when neither party checks who’s responsible. Late TDS deposit — even by a few days — carries interest under Section 201(1A) at 1.5% per month, calculated from the deduction date, not the due date, which surprises businesses who assume the interest clock starts later than it actually does.

Documents you’ll need

  • Salary register / payroll data, for Form 24Q
  • Contractor, professional fee, and rent payment ledger, for Form 26Q
  • TDS challans (ITNS 281) paid for the quarter
  • PAN details of all deductees
  • Lower deduction certificate, if obtained by any deductee
  • Previous quarter's filed return, for continuing clients

TDS/TCS compliance across Coimbatore’s industries and areas

Exposure varies sharply by sector. Jewellers in RS Puram and Big Bazaar Street face particular TDS/TCS complexity around high-value transactions and cash handling — motor vehicle and specified-goods TCS thresholds are easy to cross given typical transaction sizes in the trade, and old-gold purchases from unregistered individuals need careful treatment distinct from normal supplier payments. Textile mills and processing units on Tirupur Road and Avinashi Road, with extensive contractor and job-work payment chains, live largely in Section 194C territory, and the individual-versus-company payee rate distinction matters a great deal given how many small contractors and job-workers they engage. IT and services firms in Saravanampatti and Peelamedu deal most with Section 192 salary TDS at scale and Section 194J for consultant and technical payments, plus Section 195 withholding where payments go to non-resident vendors or contractors. Manufacturers in Singanallur and Ganapathy purchasing raw material in bulk are the group most likely to trigger Section 194Q on large single-supplier purchases, and need that checked consistently across the year, not just when a single large invoice prompts the question.

Frequently asked questions about TDS/TCS Return Filing & Compliance

What is the penalty for late filing of TDS returns?

₹200 per day under Section 234E until the return is filed, capped at the TDS amount, plus a separate penalty under Section 271H (₹10,000 to ₹1,00,000) if the delay exceeds one year or the return contains incorrect details.

What TDS rate applies to a Coimbatore business paying a contractor?

Generally 1% for individual/HUF contractors and 2% for other entities under Section 194C, provided the contractor has furnished a valid PAN; without PAN, the rate rises to 20%.

Do I need to deduct TDS on rent paid for office premises?

Yes, if the annual rent exceeds ₹2,40,000, TDS at 10% (2% for machinery/equipment rent) applies under Section 194-I; for individuals/HUFs not subject to audit paying rent above ₹50,000/month, Section 194-IB applies instead.

What is a lower/nil deduction certificate and how do I get one?

It's a certificate under Section 197 allowing tax deduction at a lower rate (or nil) where the deductee's actual tax liability is lower than the standard TDS rate would suggest; we file Form 13 with supporting income projections to obtain it.

My TDS return shows a default for PAN mismatch — how is this fixed?

We file a correction return on TRACES updating the correct PAN against the challan, which resolves the default and restores the deductee's credit in their Form 26AS.

Is TCS applicable to my business, and on what transactions?

TCS applies to specified transactions — sale of certain goods above prescribed limits, foreign remittances under LRS, overseas tour packages, and a few other categories — we assess whether any of your transactions attract it.

How often do I need to file TDS returns?

Quarterly — by 31 July, 31 October, 31 January, and 31 May for the respective quarters — followed by issuance of Form 16/16A to deductees within the prescribed period after filing.

I buy goods worth over ₹50 lakh a year from one supplier — do I deduct TDS or does the seller collect TCS?

If your turnover exceeded ₹10 crore in the preceding year, Section 194Q takes precedence — you deduct TDS at 0.1% as the buyer, and the seller should not additionally collect TCS under Section 206C(1H) on the same transaction.
